content.subtitle: General rule
content.description: If the reporting verb is in the past (e.g., said), we usually have to change the tense of what the person actually said. The general rule is to go back one tense, but there are some exceptions. With past perfect simple and continuous, there is no tense change.

content.subtitle: Tense changes table
items: Direct speech: present simple → Reported speech: past simple
Direct speech: present continuous → Reported speech: past continuous
Direct speech: present perfect simple → Reported speech: past perfect simple
Direct speech: present perfect continuous → Reported speech: past perfect continuous
Direct speech: past simple → Reported speech: past perfect simple
Direct speech: past continuous → Reported speech: past perfect continuous

content.subtitle: Examples
items: present simple: Sam **doesn't play** hockey very often. → Fiona said that Sam **didn't play** hockey very often.
present continuous: I'm **winning!** → Carol shouted to us that she was **winning.**
present perfect simple: I've never **been given** a trophy before! → Paul said that he'd **never been given** a trophy before.
present perfect continuous: They've **been playing** for four hours. → She told us that they'd **been playing** for four hours.
past simple: We **lost** the match. → Finally he told us that they had **lost** the match.
past continuous: We were **winning** until half-time. → She said they **had been winning** until half-time.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: When something is still true, we can change tense but we don't have to. When we are reporting a scientific or historical fact, we don't usually change tense.
examples: ✓ The article said that fishing **is/was** the most popular sport in Britain.
✓ Alan told me that Queen Victoria **hated** Gladstone, the Prime Minister.

sections.title: Reported speech: modal and semi-modal changes

content.subtitle: Modal changes
content.description: If the reporting verb is in the past (e.g., said), we sometimes have to change modals and semi-modals. Could, would, should, ought to, had better and need do not change.

content.subtitle: Modal changes table
items: will/shall → would
can → could
must → must / had to / was/were to / should
have to → had to
don't/doesn't have to → didn't have to
mustn't → mustn't / was/were not to / shouldn't
may → might
am/is/are going to → was/were going to

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: When expressing obligation, **must** usually changes to **had to**, **was/were to** or **should**.
When expressing obligation, **mustn't** can become **mustn't**, **was/were not to** or **shouldn't**.
**Must** doesn't usually change to **had to** or **should** when we are expressing probability.

sections.title: Reported speech: pronoun, determiner, time and place changes

content.subtitle: Pronoun and determiner changes
items: my → his/her
this/that + noun → the/that
this/that + verb → it/that
these/those + noun → the/those
these/those + verb → they
verb + these/those (without object) → them

content.subtitle: Time and place changes
items: here → there
tomorrow → the following/next day, the day after
tonight → that night
yesterday → the day before, the previous day
now, at the moment → then, at that moment
next week/year/etc → the following/next week/year, the week after
last week/year/etc → the week before, the previous week
ago → before, previously, earlier

content.subtitle: What about?
content.description: We do not need to change time words/phrases when the information is still true at the moment of speaking/writing. In narratives, writers often use direct speech time words and phrases for dramatic effect.

sections.title: Reported questions, commands, requests, and reporting verbs

content.subtitle: Reported questions
content.description: We make the same tense, pronoun, and time/place changes as with reported speech. We don't use the question form or question marks.
examples: Do you want to play Monopoly? asked Cheryl. → Cheryl asked **if/whether** we wanted to play Monopoly.
What time did the match start? asked Jimmy. → Jimmy asked Andrea **what time the match had started**.

content.subtitle: Infinitive structure
content.description: The structure 'question word + infinitive' is very common with reported questions.
examples: She asked me **how to tune a piano.**

content.subtitle: Reported commands and requests
examples: Commands: 'Put the cricket bats away!' said Alex. → Alex **told me to put** the cricket bats away.
Requests: 'Would you put the cricket bats away?' asked Alex. → Alex **asked me to put** the cricket bats away.

content.subtitle: Reporting verbs
content.description: Different reporting verbs take different grammatical patterns (e.g., deny doing, deny sth, deny that…).
examples: Common reporting verbs include: accuse, agree, apologise, ask, beg, claim, command, cry, deny, explain, instruct, order, promise, refuse, reply, respond, say, shout, state, suggest, tell, whisper.

sections.title: A: Similar Meaning Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence: Jimmy said that he definitely wasn't going to the match on Saturday.
items.answer: 'I'm definitely not going to the match on Saturday,' said Jimmy.

items.sentence: Alison told Ben that he'd forgotten to do the washing-up.
items.answer: 'You've forgotten to do the washing-up, Ben!' said Alison.

items.sentence: Jason admitted that he had been lying when he'd said he had nothing to do with the breakages.
items.answer: 'Actually, I was lying when I said I had nothing to do with the breakages,' admitted Jason.

items.sentence: Paul told me that Dawn hadn't been particularly hard-working when she was at university.
items.answer: 'Dawn wasn't particularly hard-working when she was at university,' said Paul.

items.sentence: Sharon shouted that she hadn't finished yet.
Rudy said he'd meet us all at the café in half an hour.
items.answer: 'I'll meet you all at the café in half an hour,' said Rudy.

items.sentence: Belinda's mum told her that she didn't have to eat her sprouts if she didn't want to.
items.answer: 'You don't have to eat your sprouts if you don't want to, Belinda,' said her mum.

items.sentence: Sebastian announced that he and Tristan were going to set up a website.
items.answer: 'Tristan and I are going to set up a website,' announced Sebastian.

items.sentence: Karen asked Diana when she had got married.
items.answer: 'When did you get married, Diana?' asked Karen.

items.sentence: Freda asked Bjorn if she could borrow his bike for a couple of hours.
items.answer: 'Bjorn, can I borrow your bike for a couple of hours?' asked Freda.

sections.title: B: Multiple Choice (Tense Changes)
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: When I told Mr Peters that the dog **has / had** eaten my homework, he didn't believe me.
Adam told me that his girlfriend **doesn't / didn't** eat meat at all.
When I saw Ed yesterday morning, he told me that he is **/ was** going to look at a flat in the afternoon.
Roger told me about the pop quiz. He said his team **were / had been** doing well in the first few rounds, but that it **all went / had all gone** wrong with the round about dance music.
When we watched *Death Watch III*, Paul said he **has / had** never seen a worse movie in his life!
Did Galileo say that the Earth **goes / went** round the sun?
Phil told me on Friday that he **has / had** just bumped into Wally Deacon!
Babs announced to us all last night that she is **/ was** finally giving up smoking.
Did you say that you **do / did** want sugar in your coffee or not?
I told Brian last week that the office **isn't / wasn't** opening on Tuesday so I don't know why he showed up yesterday morning.

sections.title: C: Error Correction (Modal Changes)
sections.instructions: If the word or phrase in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: Bob did tell me that he probably **wouldn't be** coming in today.
Last week he told us that we **don't have to** do that exercise and then yesterday he said **he'd made** a mistake.
Did you say these bills **had to** be paid before tomorrow?
Steve said he **could** play guitar when he was two years old. Do you believe that?
Pauline told my mum that **she'd had better** get a lawyer. I think she's right.
When we went to the party, Claudia told me that **I must be** on my best behaviour all evening. It was very rude of her, I thought.
Darren said that Mr Taylor **was to** have forgotten about the meeting, but I'm not so sure. I think he just didn't want to come.
When I was a teenager, my dad always told me that **I'm not to** stay out later than ten o'clock.
I was pleased when Jilly told us that she **can** help us paint the flat. If only I'd known then what that meant!
When I was at school, my English teacher always told us that we **don't have to** pay attention but we **do have to be quiet.**
items.answer: didn't have to, had to be quiet.

items.sentence: The letter they sent six months ago said that they **shall** be in touch again soon. Well, I haven't heard anything since!
Seven years ago, the Prime Minister stated that his government **will** be corruption-free. It doesn't look that way now, does it?

sections.title: D: Fill in the Gap (Pronouns and Determiners)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: I actually really liked the shoes but Maureen told me …………… didn't go with the suit I was planning to wear.
Susan told us in no uncertain terms that ………………… play was rubbish.
Why did Derek say that …………………… sister's called Chloe? She's called Clarissa, isn't she?
Mike told the waiter – quite rudely in my opinion – that ………………… wasn't what he'd ordered.
My mum said that …………………… trainers were too expensive so I didn't get them in the end.
She did actually tell me …………………… number but I stupidly forgot to write it down.
Mum asked me to put …………………… on the table.
Angus and James told me that …………………… house was broken into last night.

sections.title: E: Fill in the Gap (Time and Place Changes)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap, based on the conversation provided.

items.sentence: Seamus asked Marios what he was doing at ………………… moment.
Marios replied that right ………………… he was not doing anything much.
Seamus mentioned a conversation they'd had the ………………… week.
Marios admitted that he'd been to the gym two days ………………… .
Marios reminded Seamus that he was taking part in the London Marathon ………………… month.
Marios told Seamus that he had been planning to call him the day ………………… .
Seamus said he was at the office and suggested they meet ………………… .

sections.title: F: Similar Meaning Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence: 'Carol and I went to a great museum when we were in Italy last month,' said Shirley.
items.answer: Shirley told her friends that she and Carol had been to a great museum when they were in Italy the previous month.

items.sentence: 'I only washed these trousers yesterday, Tony!' said his mum.
items.answer: Tony's mum told him she had only washed those/the trousers the day before.

items.sentence: 'You have to get all the letters sent off by tomorrow,' said Jake to his secretary as he was leaving.
items.answer: As he was leaving, Jake told his secretary she had to get all the letters sent off by the next day.

items.sentence: 'We shall meet you all back here in exactly half an hour from now.'
items.answer: Mum told me that they would meet us all back there in exactly half an hour from then.

items.sentence: 'Luckily I don't have to go into the office this Saturday,' said Olivia.
items.answer: Olivia said happily that luckily she didn't have to go into the office that Saturday.

items.sentence: 'It can't be very nice having all these cars going by all the time.'
items.answer: Alan commented to Ros that it couldn't be very nice having all those cars going by all the time.

items.sentence: 'I'll put these in water right now.'
items.answer: Daniel's mum said that she would put those in water right then.

items.sentence: 'I told Dan two days ago that this report would have to be rewritten,' said Greg.
items.answer: Greg told Jeanne that he had told Dan two days before that that report would have to be rewritten.

sections.title: G: Keyword Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: Will you come with me to the concert next Thursday? Andrea asked Gareth. go
items.answer: Andrea asked Gareth if he would go with her to the concert the following Thursday.

items.sentence: 'Does the train get in at four o'clock?' asked Pete. whether
items.answer: Pete asked whether the train got in at four o'clock.

items.sentence: 'Have you been talking on the phone all evening, Tim?' asked his dad. if
items.answer: Tim's dad asked if he had been talking on the phone all evening.

items.sentence: 'Are you all coming tomorrow?' Fiona asked us. whether
items.answer: Fiona asked whether we were all coming the next day.

items.sentence: 'Doesn't Jerry live near you anymore?' asked Dominic. me
items.answer: Dominic asked me whether Jerry didn't live near me any more.

items.sentence: 'Shall I send you an e-mail about it, Trevor?' asked Kevin. him
items.answer: Kevin asked Trevor if he should send him an e-mail about it.

items.sentence: 'Shall I help you do the dishes, Carlo?' said Robert. offered
items.answer: Robert offered to help Carlo (to) do the dishes.

items.sentence: 'Do we really have to go to bed right now?' Toby asked his parents. did
items.answer: Toby asked his parents if they really did have to go to bed right then.

sections.title: H: Multiple Choice (Question Word Order)
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Kostas didn't dare ask Eleni where **had she / she had** been the previous evening.
I was tempted to ask why **did Ranji want / Ranji wanted** to sit next to Li Han so badly.
We did ask Carol who **she had / had she** invited but for some reason she wouldn't tell us.
Can you believe it? Fred actually asked me how **to / could he** send an e-mail!
No one has asked me why **didn't I / I didn't** come to work yesterday.
I'm going to ask them what on earth **did they think / they thought** they were doing.
Ask that man over there who **should we / to** complain to.
Doug and Sally didn't even ask me how **the website was / was the website** going.

sections.title: I: Verb Patterns (Infinitive/Gerund)
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: You'd better ask the security guard how ………………… into the building at the weekend.
That was so nice of them to ask whether I ………………………… to stay the night.
I know you asked me ……………………………… the washing-up now but it'll only take a minute, I promise.
Why did the officer order his men ……………………… when he knew they were outnumbered?
I would ask you …………………………… judgement until you know all the facts.
I couldn't believe it when Sandy came out and asked me if I ……………………… drugs before.
I don't like asking you ……………………… on a Saturday, but I'm afraid there's no alternative.
You should have asked your mother which cloth ……………………….. She'd know.
It was funny when you were asked by the technician how ……………………… the computer on.
She very kindly asked me if I ……………………… fruit cake and said there was a fruit salad if I preferred.
Go and ask your dad ……………………… us a coffee, would you?
I'm sorry, but we've been instructed …………………………… to reporters under any circumstances.
Do you deny ………………… this with the defendant on the day in question?
Why did you refuse ………………… the police officer's questions? It only makes you look guilty!
It's about time Mrs Richards apologised to me ………………… me a gossip in front of everybody.
She may claim ………………… a PhD but nobody's ever actually seen the certificate.
The kids suggested ………………… a pizza tonight. What do you think?
I do agree there ………………… simple solution to this problem.
Tammy responded ………………… that at least she'd never lied to her parents.
I'm begging ………………… .

sections.title: K: Fill in the Gap (Reporting Verbs in Context)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: The next time someone asks me (1) ...... my hobbies are, or, indeed, (2) ...... I have any hobbies, I'm tempted to refuse (3) ...... answer.
Better still, I'll turn the question round and ask them to (4) ...... me what their hobbies are.
Doubtless they'll claim to (5) ...... an avid collector of rare postage stamps, or an expert builder-of-cathedrals-out-of-matchsticks, but I shan't believe it.
I don't think we should deny (6) ...... any longer: the hobby is dead.
I would like to suggest (7) ...... , from now on, the word 'hobby' is erased from our vocabulary.
You may accuse me (8) ...... being naive, but I strongly believe the facts support my case.
Ask any teenager – or adult, for that matter – how they (9) ...... their spare time.

They'll respond (10) ...... a list of activities – going to the gym, playing computer games, watching TV, downloading music, hanging out with friends, surfing the Internet, going to the pub, playing football – none of which can sensibly be called a hobby.
